items.headline: Disappearance Of An Analytical Chemist After A Lecture In Albemarle-Street
items.body: Considerable interest has been occasioned in professional circles by the unexplained disappearance of Dr. Edwin Harcourt, analytical chemist, of Craven-street, Strand, who, it will be remembered, delivered a lecture on certain disinfectant preparations at the Royal Institution on Tuesday evening. Dr. Harcourt has not since been seen, and when his laboratory was entered on the following morning the rooms were found in much disorder. The police, whilst making full inquiry, state that no body has been discovered, and that at present they are unable to speak with certainty as to whether foul play has been committed.

items.headline: Passage To New York. S.S. Mercia.
items.body: A few berths still remain disengaged on board the S.S. Mercia, appointed to sail on Thursday evening from the London Docks. Limited room is also available for select cargo. Intending passengers and shippers should apply to the dock agents before four o'clock.

items.headline: Wanted, A Night Porter.
items.body: Rudd and Vale require, for night duty at their eastern warehouse, a sober and trustworthy man acquainted with chemical cargo. Written references of a satisfactory character will be required. Applications to be made at the Fenchurch-street office before noon.

items.headline: Turf Commission Agent Before The Magistrate.
items.body: At the sitting yesterday Ezra Pike, described as a turf commission agent, of Cranbourn-street, was proceeded against for the irregular condition of his books, and was fined. Evidence was given to the effect that several young clerks and assistants had within recent weeks lost heavily through transactions carried on at the office.

items.headline: Science And Society.
items.body: Lady Ashcombe was among those who entertained a small company after Tuesday's lecture at the Royal Institution. Dr. Harcourt's observations upon inexpensive antiseptic vapours are understood to have attracted more than ordinary attention in certain commercial quarters.

items.headline: Shipping Intelligence.
items.body: The S.S. Mercia is announced to receive, weather permitting, manufactured goods, laboratory glass, and two crates of chemical samples for New York. Final manifests close this afternoon.

items.headline: Charing Cross Ticket Office.
items.body: Owing to a temporary rearrangement of the counters at Charing Cross the booking clerks have during the present week been obliged to keep their ticket ledgers with unusual exactness.

items.headline: The Smoke Evil.
items.body:
Sir,-It is idle to expect that London shall breathe cleanly while every factory chimney is allowed to discharge its burden over the poorer thoroughfares.

If science can offer a remedy, Parliament ought not to be slow in hearing it.

-I am, Sir, &c.

items.headline: At The Lyceum.
items.body: A crowded house is announced for Saturday at the Lyceum. Gentlemen are particularly requested not to obstruct the carriage entrance on leaving after the performance.

items.headline: Lost, Mislaid, Or Stolen.
items.body: A silver watch, plain case, bearing the initials E.H., is reported missing from rooms in the neighbourhood of the Strand. Pawnbrokers and others are requested to communicate without delay with the owner.

items.headline: Furnished Lodgings For A Gentleman.
items.body: To be let, in a quiet house near Russell-square, a well-aired first-floor sitting-room, with bed-room adjoining, attendance if desired. Terms moderate. No objection to a student or member of the medical profession.

items.headline: Wanted, A Useful General Servant.
items.body: Wanted, where a maid is kept, a respectable young woman, able to cook plainly and willing to assist in the laundry. Good character indispensable. Apply, by letter only, N.W. district.

items.headline: Sale By Auction.
items.body: On Monday next, the whole of the neat household furniture and effects of a family removing from Camberwell-grove, including mahogany dining-table, eight chairs in hair seating, Brussels carpets, bedsteads, and plated ware.

items.headline: Church Intelligence.
items.body: At St. Martin's-in-the-Fields, on Sunday next, a sermon will be preached in aid of the Metropolitan Nursing Fund. Collections after morning and evening service.

items.headline: Cheap Excursion To Brighton.
items.body: The South Coast Company announce for Saturday a cheap excursion to Brighton, leaving London Bridge at 8.20 a.m., and returning at 10.15 p.m. Third-class return fare, 4s. 6d.

items.headline: Gravesend Departures.
items.body: The outward vessels cleared yesterday include the Iris for Rotterdam, the Helene for Antwerp, and the Mabel for Havre. Wind moderate from the westward.

items.headline: Painless Dentistry.
items.body: Artificial teeth, stopping, and extractions by a new method. Consultations from ten till six. Moderate fees. Established twenty years.

items.headline: Clerk Wanted.
items.body: Merchant in the City requires a junior clerk accustomed to invoice work and a clear commercial hand. State age, salary expected, and whether resident with parents.

items.headline: Alleged Pickpocket At Marylebone.
items.body: At the Marylebone Police-court a young woman was charged with stealing a purse from a visitor to the Zoological Gardens. The accused denied the offence, and was remanded.

items.headline: A Horticultural Gathering.
items.body: The spring exhibition at South Kensington was yesterday attended by a fashionable company, several conservatories being specially admired for their orchids and early roses.

items.headline: Crossing-Sweepers And The Streets.
items.body:
Sir,-There is no more melancholy spectacle in wet weather than the industrious crossing-sweeper driven from his stand by rough lads who neither labour themselves nor will suffer others to do so.

-I am, Sir, your obedient servant,

A Householder.

items.headline: A Disputed Passage In A Campaign Memoir.
items.body:
Sir,-The communication of 'An Old Subaltern,' which appeared in your issue of yesterday, leaves untouched the precise matter raised by those officers who have challenged Captain Lethbridge's recently-published narrative.

Your correspondent invites the public to suppose that the objections now taken are directed merely against trifling blemishes of recollection; but that is not the contention at all. The point insisted upon is that a particular incident, described with much dramatic minuteness, and so described as to lend colour and authority to the whole chapter in which it appears, did not occur in the manner stated, and could not have occurred under the conditions then existing.

Captain Lethbridge now says, in effect, that a date may have slipped his memory, or that an hour may have been wrongly set down. If the matter were no more than that, no officer of experience would have troubled the press with contradiction. But when a writer places himself in a scene from which other men, still living, distinctly remember him to have been absent, the question ceases to be one of literary ornament and becomes one of fact.

I was myself attached at the time to the advanced post to which reference has been made, and I have before me notes written on the following day, besides a private letter from an officer then serving on the same line. Neither in my own memorandum, nor in his, is there the smallest trace of the interview so confidently narrated in Captain Lethbridge's volume. Had such an occurrence taken place it is hardly conceivable that it should have escaped immediate remark among those on duty there.

No one desires to deny to a gallant officer the credit of honourable service; but it is surely open to those who bore a part in the same operations to protest when embellishment is offered to the public as evidence. A campaign diary may contain slips; it must not contain inventions. Until the original statement is either withdrawn or supported by something better than general assertion, the criticism already passed upon it will remain unanswered.

-I am, Sir, your obedient servant,

H. M. Arden,
Late Captain, 3rd Battalion.

Queen's-gate, May 16.

items.headline: At The Alhambra.
items.body: A new ballet, with novel electrical effects, is announced for this evening, together with the usual variety entertainment. Doors open at seven.

items.headline: Fog On The Lower Thames.
items.body: Navigation below Greenwich was in some degree impeded during the earlier hours of yesterday by a thick river fog, though no serious casualty has been reported.

items.headline: Gun Club Meeting At Hendon.
items.body: Under bright skies a well-attended meeting was held yesterday under the auspices of the Metropolitan Gun Club at Hendon, several handicaps attracting good entries. The principal event, a five-bird competition for a cup value 20 guineas, was secured by Mr. A. L. Fenwick, who grassed five birds in six shots, while Mr. C. Branscombe and Mr. H. Veasey divided the next honours with four each. A later sweepstake was shared by two competitors who each accounted for their birds in capital style. The trapping, though somewhat irregular in the earlier rounds, was on the whole pronounced satisfactory.

items.headline: Great Drapery Clearance.
items.body: Black silks, mourning crepes, ladies' mantles, and children's pelisses at very reduced prices for ready money. Country orders carefully attended to.

items.headline: Delay To Cargo At Wapping.
items.body: Some inconvenience was occasioned yesterday at several warehouses eastward of the Tower by a dispute amongst lightermen; but cargo intended for outward foreign steamers continued to be received under special arrangement.

items.headline: Births, Deaths, And Marriages.
items.body:
Births.-On the 15th inst., at 22, Holland-park-gardens, the wife of Arthur Lennox, of a daughter. On the 14th inst., at Clapham-rise, the wife of Mr. Septimus Dale, solicitor, of a son.

Marriages.-On the 16th inst., at St. Jude's, Kensington, Mr. Percival Moore, youngest son of the late Mr. Henry Moore, of Bayswater, to Edith, second daughter of Mr. James Waller, of Campden-hill-road. On the 12th inst., at St. Michael's, Chester-square, Mr. Alfred Penson, of the Inner Temple, to Mabel, eldest daughter of Dr. Redford, of Onslow-gardens.

Deaths.-On the 16th inst., at Pembridge-square, Alice Marian, widow of the late Capt. F. H. Wroughton, aged 63. On the 15th inst., at Streatham, infant daughter of Mr. and Mrs. Lionel Brooke. On the 14th inst., at Highbury, Mrs. Eleanor Fitch, relict of the late Samuel Fitch, aged 71.
